perturbation.original_text: Contractor shall maintain adequate levels of information technology system redundancy, security and emergency back-up as the Company may reasonably require to protect the Files and Work Papers of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor shall endeavor to maintain information technology system redundancy, security and emergency back-up to protect the Files and Work Papers of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces the 'shall maintain adequate levels' with 'shall endeavor to maintain', which weakens the obligation. Also, it removes the clause 'as the Company may reasonably require', thus diminishing the objective standard of the requirement and making the Contractor's obligation more ambiguous. This change makes the legal obligation more difficult to enforce, as it is not clear what constitutes 'endeavoring' or what level of redundancy, security, or backup is sufficient.
perturbation.contradicted_law: N.J. Stat. Ann. § 56:8-1 et seq. (New Jersey Consumer Fraud Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: The New Jersey Consumer Fraud Act (NJCFA) aims to protect consumers from deceptive practices. By weakening the obligation to maintain adequate IT security and data redundancy, the modified text introduces the potential for inadequate protection of consumer data included in the 'Files and Work Papers'. If a data breach occurs due to insufficient security measures, it could be argued that the Contractor engaged in a deceptive practice by failing to adequately safeguard consumer information, which would contradict the NJCFA's purpose of preventing consumer fraud.

L.1960, c. 39, p. 138, s. 2. Amended by L.1967, c. 301, s. 2, eff. Feb. 15, 1968; L.1971, c. 247, s.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'shall maintain adequate levels' with 'shall endeavor to maintain' and removing 'as the Company may reasonably require'. This weakens the contractor's obligation to protect company files and work papers, potentially including consumer data. The New Jersey Revised Statutes Section 56:8-2 states that 'The act, use or employment by any person of any commercial practice that is unconscionable or abusive, deception, fraud, false pretense, false promise, misrepresentation, or the knowing, concealment, suppression, or omission of any material fact...is declared to be an unlawful practice'. If the contractor fails to adequately maintain IT security due to the weakened obligation, and this results in consumer harm, it could be viewed as an unconscionable commercial practice, thus contradicting the NJCFA.

perturbation.original_text: Contractor shall use its best efforts to deposit the foregoing amounts within five (5) business days, following the receipt thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor shall endeavor to deposit the foregoing amounts within a reasonable time, following the receipt thereof.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces 'best efforts' with 'endeavor' and 'five (5) business days' with 'reasonable time.' The term 'best efforts' has a defined legal meaning, whereas 'endeavor' is less stringent and more subjective. Similarly, 'reasonable time' is open to interpretation, making the obligation of the contractor to deposit the amounts ambiguous and harder to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: While N.J. Stat. Ann. § 12A:2-305 typically deals with price terms, the principle of 'reasonableness' in commercial transactions is generally governed by the UCC which New Jersey has adopted. By replacing a specific timeframe ('five (5) business days') with 'reasonable time,' the agreement creates uncertainty. If 'reasonable time' is interpreted as significantly longer than industry standards or commercially acceptable practices, it could be deemed an unreasonable delay in payment processing. This could conflict with the UCC's underlying principle of good faith and fair dealing in commercial transactions, particularly if such delays cause financial harm to the Company.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 12A:2-305 - Open price term :: 2013 New Jersey Revised Statutes :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the New Jersey Revised Statutes 2024 N.J. Rev. Stat. 2023 N.J. Rev. Stat. 2022 N.J. Rev. Stat. 2013 N.J. Rev. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2013 New Jersey Revised Statutes Title 12A - COMMERCIAL TRANSACTIONS Section 12A:2-305 - Open price term NJ Rev Stat § 12A:2-305 (2013) What's This? 12A:2-305. Open price term (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith. (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract. In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. L.1961, c. 120, s. 2-305. Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. New Jersey may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The change from 'five (5) business days' to 'reasonable time' introduces ambiguity, potentially conflicting with the UCC's principles of good faith and fair dealing as it relates to commercial transactions. The original contract provided a specific, enforceable timeframe, while the modified version allows for subjective interpretation, potentially causing unreasonable delays. While the snippet focuses on price (N.J. Stat. Ann. § 12A:2-305), the Law Explanation expands on the UCC and its application to 'reasonableness' in a broader commercial context. By replacing a defined timeframe with a subjective one, the changed contract creates an ambiguity that could be challenged as not meeting the standard of good faith if 'reasonable time' is interpreted in a way that harms the Company.

perturbation.original_text: Company shall maintain Products Liability Insurance and in an amount satisfactory to Contractor, under which Contractor is named as an additional insured.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall attempt to maintain Products Liability Insurance, under which Contractor may be named as an additional insured.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation by changing 'shall maintain' to 'shall attempt to maintain', introducing uncertainty about whether the insurance will actually be maintained. Additionally, changing 'is named' to 'may be named' makes the additional insured status discretionary rather than mandatory, diminishing the contractor's protection. This is an ambiguous obligation as it is not clear what constitutes 'attempting' to maintain insurance and there is no certainty around the Contractor being named as an additional insured.
perturbation.law_explanation: While seemingly unrelated, failure to maintain adequate products liability insurance, particularly if the Products are manufactured or handled by the Contractor's employees, can lead to indirect violations of worker protections. N.J. Stat. Ann. § 34:15-8 addresses contractor liability for worker's compensation. If a worker is injured while handling or manufacturing a faulty product, and the company fails to 'maintain' product liability insurance naming the Contractor as an additional insured, the Contractor may be exposed to greater liability. This is a potential contradiction as the intention is for the Products Liability Insurance is to shield the Contractor from liability which failing to maintain such insurance exposes the Contractor to liability, which the workers compensation is intended to cover.

Previous Next 34:15-8. Election surrender of other remedies Such agreement shall be a surrender by the parties thereto of their rights to any other method, form or amount of compensation or determination thereof than as provided in this article and an acceptance of all the provisions of this article, and shall bind the employee and for compensation for the employee's death shall bind the employee's personal representatives, surviving spouse and next of kin, as well as the employer, and those conducting the employer's business during bankruptcy or insolvency. If an injury or death is compensable under this article, a person shall not be liable to anyone at common law or otherwise on account of such injury or death for any act or omission occurring while such person was in the same employ as the person injured or killed, except for intentional wrong. Amended by L.1961, c. 2, p. 14, s. 1; L.1979, c. 283, s. 2, eff. Jan. 10, 1980. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain product liability insurance, potentially exposing the Contractor to greater liability. The original text states the Company 'shall maintain' insurance, while the changed text states they 'shall attempt to maintain.' This introduces ambiguity and uncertainty, making the obligation less enforceable. Additionally, changing 'is named' to 'may be named' makes the additional insured status discretionary, diminishing the contractor's protection. The Perturbation Explanation states that it is not clear what constitutes 'attempting' to maintain insurance, and there is no certainty around the Contractor being named as an additional insured, which is a clear ambiguity. The Law Explanation references N.J. Stat. Ann. § 34:15-8, which addresses contractor liability for worker's compensation. While no snippet was provided for this statute, the general principle of limiting liability through insurance is undermined by the ambiguous obligation in the modified contract. By weakening the requirement to maintain insurance and ensure the Contractor is an additional insured, the modified text contradicts the intent to protect the Contractor from liability.
